why.1: The delivered code block has no commented-out old loader; the prose states the unused load() block was dropped, and the module starts at imports + ld/snd only.
why.2: Both handlers still use bare except: (in ld when loading config, and in snd on request failure); no exception types are named.
why.3: ld still does except: d = {} on any load failure, so missing/unparseable config silently becomes empty defaults with no raise or ERROR log naming the file/problem.
why.4: Cryptic names remain throughout: ld, snd, p, d, r, to, pl (and u); none were replaced with descriptive identifiers.
why.5: Defaults stay inline literals: d.get("retries", 5), d.get("timeout", 30), d.get("backoff_seconds", 0) and backoff=0; no module-level named constants.
why.6: No top-of-file header about role/constraints; the module still has the restating '# get the retries' comment after the bare except.

why.3: ld() still catches all failures and assigns d = {}, silently falling through to defaults instead of raising or ERROR-logging with the file path and problem.
why.4: Cryptic names are unchanged throughout: ld, snd, p, d, r, to, pl (plus u, bo). No descriptive renames appear.

why.1: The delivered module still includes the full commented-out old loader block (`# old loader, kept for reference` plus the commented `load` function). Expectation required that block be removed, not preserved.

why.1: The delivered module has no commented-out old loader; the reference load() block is absent from the code shown.
why.2: Handlers use named types only: (OSError, json.JSONDecodeError) and (urllib.error.URLError, OSError); no bare except: appears.

why.4: ld/snd/p/d/r/to/pl are gone; names are load_config, send_notification, path, config, retries, timeout, payload, backoff_seconds, etc.

why.1: The delivered module code has no commented-out old loader (`# def load(f):` / `# old loader`) block; that dead code is absent from the proposed `notifier.py`.
why.2: Both handlers name types: `except (OSError, json.JSONDecodeError)` and `except (urllib.error.URLError, OSError)`. No bare `except:` appears in the module.
why.3: On load failure the code only does `logger.warning(...)` then `config = {}` and continues with defaults. The expectation requires an explicit error via raise or an ERROR-level log naming the file and problem; WARNING plus fall-through does not satisfy that.
why.4: Cryptic names are gone: `ld`/`snd` → `notifier_load_config`/`notifier_send`; `p`/`d`/`r`/`to`/`pl` → `config_path`/`config`/`retries`/`timeout_seconds`/`payload` (and similar descriptive names elsewhere).
why.5: Defaults are module-level constants (`DEFAULT_RETRIES = 5`, `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 30`, `DEFAULT_BACKOFF_SECONDS = 0`); function bodies use those names, not inline `5`/`30`/`0`.
why.6: Module opens with a role/design-constraints header docstring; there are no `# get the retries`-style comments and no header/inline text that only restates what the next line of code already shows.

why.1: The delivered module source has no commented-out old loader (no `# old loader` / `# def load(f)` block); only the new `config_load` path remains.
why.2: Both handlers name types: `(OSError, json.JSONDecodeError)` and `(urllib.error.URLError, OSError)`. No bare `except:` appears in the module.
why.3: On load failure the code logs at WARNING (`logger.warning(...)`) then still sets `config = {}` and returns defaults. Expectation requires raise or ERROR-level log naming file and problem, not fall-through to an empty dict; WARNING + defaults fails that bar.
why.4: Cryptic names are gone: `ld`/`snd` → `config_load`/`notification_send`; params/locals use `config_path`, `config`, `retries`, `timeout_seconds`, `payload`, `url`, `attempt`, etc. None of ld/snd/p/d/r/to/pl remain.
why.5: Defaults are module-level `DEFAULT_RETRIES = 5`, `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 30`, `DEFAULT_BACKOFF_SECONDS = 0`; function bodies only reference those names via `.get(...)`, not inline 5/30/0 as defaults.
why.6: Top module docstring states role and design constraints. No `# get the retries`-style comments; remaining text is role/constraints and operational log messages, not restating adjacent code.

why.1: Finding 1 explicitly states status and refunded_amount are mutated before payments_api.refund() with no rollback, so a failed/crashed refund can leave the order marked refunded; that is the pre-success state-mutation defect.
why.2: Findings 2 and 4 cover the non-ok path: result status != ok is ignored, the function still returns True, and order.fee is set on a failed/rejected refund—i.e. a failed API result is treated as success.
why.3: Finding 5 flags that amount is never validated, citing no positivity check and no upper bound against what was paid, so invalid amounts can reach the payment API.
why.4: Finding 7 notes logs omit order_id/amount/exc_info, but the fix is only the gesture “add context + exc_info=True”—no concrete improved log message (e.g. a sample log.error with order_id, amount, payment_ref, and exception detail).
why.5: Finding 6 states orders[order_id] raises an unhandled KeyError for unknown ids.
why.6: The closing fix list proposes validate amount, reorder state mutation after success confirmation, and (with finding 2) not treating non-ok as success—i.e. validate, call the API, then mutate only after a confirmed-ok result.

why.1: Finding #1 explicitly states order.status/refunded_amount are set before payments_api.refund(), and a raised call leaves the order permanently refunded with no money moved; fix is to mutate only after confirmed "ok".
why.2: Finding #2 states that when result status is not "ok" the code only logs a warning, still sets order.fee, and returns True; fix is return False and skip fee/status changes.
why.3: Finding #4 flags no amount validation and that negative, zero, or over-limit amounts reach the payment API and fee calc, with a concrete guard (amount <= 0 or amount > order.amount).
why.4: Finding #6 only flags "refund error" as lacking exception/order_id/amount context and gives an improved log.exception line. "refund not ok" is not called out for missing diagnostic context (finding #2 criticizes success return, not log content); an improved warning appears only in the rewrite, not as a stated defect for that message.
why.5: Finding #5 explicitly flags unhandled KeyError on unknown order_id via unguarded orders[order_id], with orders.get + None check as the fix.
why.6: The suggested corrected flow validates order existence, already-refunded, and amount first; calls payments_api.refund; returns False on exception/non-ok; mutates status/refunded_amount/fee only after confirmed ok.

why.1: Defect #1 explicitly flags that status and refunded_amount are set before payments_api.refund, so a raised failure leaves the order falsely refunded, and the fix requires mutating only after result status is ok.
why.2: Defect #2 states a non-ok result only logs a warning then falls through to set fee and return True, so callers treat a failed refund as success; fix is return False before mutating order.
why.3: Defect #4 flags no amount validation (no amount > 0 or amount <= refundable balance), notes negative and over-charge can reach the API, and proposes 0 < amount <= order.total - already_refunded before the API call.
why.4: Defects #7–#8 call out bare 'refund error' / 'refund not ok' with no order_id, amount, payment_ref, or exception detail, and give concrete replacements including log.exception(... order_id, amount) and log.warning(... order_id, result).
why.5: Defect #6 explicitly names uncaught KeyError from orders[order_id] and proposes orders.get with a False return when missing.
why.6: Suggested corrected implementation validates order existence, status, and amount first, then calls the API, then mutates status/refunded_amount/fee only after confirmed-ok result.

why.1: Item 1 explicitly flags that order.status and order.refunded_amount are set before payments_api.refund, so a raised exception returns False while the order stays permanently marked refunded; fix requires mutating only after success.
why.2: Item 2 flags that non-"ok" status is only logged with a warning, then the code still sets order.fee and returns True, treating a rejected refund as success; fix is return False and skip mutation.
why.3: Item 3 flags that amount is unchecked for positive/numeric/refundable balance and gives a concrete boundary check (type, <=0, exceeding refundable_amount) before the API call.
why.4: Item 4 names both bare messages ('refund error' / 'refund not ok') as lacking order_id, payment_ref, amount, and exception detail, and supplies a concrete improved log.error format string with those fields.
why.5: Item 3 states that orders[order_id] raises an uncaught KeyError for an unknown order and proposes orders.get with an explicit error return.
why.6: Across items 1–3 the review proposes the full corrected order: validate inputs first (#3), call the API and only then mutate status/refunded_amount/fee after confirmed success (#1), and return False without mutation on non-ok (#2), with concrete code for each step.

why.1: Finding 1 explicitly calls out that order.status and order.refunded_amount are set on lines 10–11 before payments_api.refund() runs, so a raised exception leaves the order permanently marked refunded, and the fix is to move those assignments after a confirmed "ok" result.
why.2: Finding 2 states that a non-"ok" status only logs a warning then falls through to charge a fee and return True, treating a failed refund as success, and the fix is to return False in that branch.
why.3: Finding 4 states amount is never checked for being positive/numeric/within the order total, and the suggested fix validates isinstance(...), amount <= 0, and amount > order.total before any API call.
why.4: Finding 5 flags log.error("refund error") for missing order_id, amount, and exception detail and gives a concrete improved format string; the suggested fix also upgrades "refund not ok" to include order_id, amount, and api_status.
why.5: Finding 4 explicitly notes that orders[order_id] raises an unhandled, unlogged KeyError for unknown IDs, with a fix to validate order_id at the top.
why.6: The suggested rewrite validates order_id/amount (and already-refunded) first, then calls the API and checks status, and only then assigns order.status, refunded_amount, and fee—exactly the required ordering.

why.1: Item 1 explicitly flags that order.status and order.refunded_amount are set before payments_api.refund(), so a raised/failed refund leaves the order falsely marked refunded; fix says mutate only after confirmed ok.
why.2: Item 2 states that non-ok result is only warned, then falls through to set order.fee and return True, so a failed provider refund is treated as success; fix requires return False without state change.
why.3: Item 5 flags no amount validation: must be positive/non-zero and not exceed refundable/paid amount, else it goes to the API and is recorded; fix validates 0 < amount <= order.refundable_amount first. Non-numeric is not named, but boundary validation of amount is clearly called out with concrete cases.
why.4: Item 6 calls out bare 'refund error' / 'refund not ok' lacking order_id, amount, exception, or API result, and gives concrete improved log lines with those fields and exc_info=True.
why.5: Item 4 flags orders[order_id] raising unhandled KeyError when the id is missing, and proposes orders.get with log + False or OrderNotFoundError.
why.6: The suggested corrected shape validates order/status/amount first, then calls the API, treats non-ok as failure, and only then sets status, refunded_amount, and fee—matching validate → API → mutate-on-success.

why.1: The except (ValueError, TypeError) body is only `continue` with no logging, so exceptions are still silently discarded under the expectation's rule.
why.2: Rejected lines are not logged at all; there is no WARN/ERROR log of the line/sku or rejection reason.
why.3: try/except wraps only the per-line body and continues the loop, so valid later lines still run after a bad line.
why.4: `prices[sku] = float(price)` runs only after successful split and float; on exception the handler continues without writing, so failed lines leave prices unchanged.
why.5: No use of the logging module (or any failure reporting); failures are only swallowed with continue.

why.1: except ValueError body is only continue with no logging — silent discard of the exception.
why.2: No log call at all; rejected lines are not reported with line/sku content or rejection reason.
why.3: try/except is inside the for-loop with continue, so a bad line is skipped and later valid lines still run.
why.4: prices[sku] = float(price) sits inside the try after both parse steps; on ValueError nothing is written to prices.
why.5: No use of the logging module (or any leveled logger); failures are not reported at all, only continue.

why.1: The only handler is `except ValueError: continue` with no logging or other side effect. That is an except body of only continue, which the expectation treats as silent discard (even though it is not a bare except).
why.2: The except path only `continue`s. There is no WARN/ERROR log and no inclusion of the offending line/sku or a rejection reason.
why.3: try/except wraps each iteration; on ValueError the loop continues, so later valid lines still run and update prices.
why.4: `prices[sku] = float(price)` sits inside the try after both parse steps, so a failed split or float never assigns; the dict is left unchanged for that line.
why.5: No `logging` import or logger call at any level; failures are not reported via the logging module (and not via print either).

why.1: Except is `except ValueError as err:` (not bare) and the body logs via `logger.warning(...)`; it is not pass/continue alone.
why.2: `logger.warning` logs the raw line (`%r` on `line`) and the rejection reason (`err`), so each bad line is diagnosable at WARN.
why.3: try/except wraps only the per-line parse/assign inside the for-loop; on ValueError the loop continues, so valid later lines still apply.
why.4: Both unpack and `prices[sku] = float(price)` sit inside the try; on failure the assignment never runs, so prices is unchanged for that line.
why.5: Uses `logging.getLogger` and `logger.warning` (a logging level), not print.

why.1: The except is specific `(ValueError, TypeError)`, not bare `except:`. The handler body is `logger.warning(...)`, not only `pass`/`continue` with no logging.
why.2: Rejected lines are logged with `logger.warning` (WARN). The message includes the offending line via `%r` and states it was skipped as malformed, which is enough to diagnose the vendor line from logs.
why.3: The try/except wraps only the per-line body inside the `for line in feed_lines` loop. On failure it logs and continues to the next line; the batch is not aborted.
why.4: `prices[sku] = float(price)` runs only after a successful split and only if `float(price)` succeeds. Any ValueError/TypeError from split or float leaves `prices` unchanged for that line.
why.5: Reporting uses `import logging`, `logging.getLogger(__name__)`, and `logger.warning(...)` — not `print`.

why.1: The except is specific `(ValueError, TypeError)` (not bare `except:`), and the body logs via `logger.error(...)` rather than only `pass`/`continue`.
why.2: Rejected lines are logged at ERROR with the offending line (`%r` / `line`) and the rejection reason (`err`).
why.3: try/except wraps only the per-line parse/assign inside the `for line in feed_lines` loop, so a bad line is skipped and the loop continues applying remaining valid lines.
why.4: `prices[sku] = float(price)` is inside the try; if split or float fails, the assignment never runs, so prices is unchanged for that line.
why.5: Uses `import logging`, a module logger, and `logger.error(...)` (a logging level), not `print`.

why.1: OUTPUT code uses bare payload["event_type"] / payload["invoice"] / invoice["id"] with no boundary checks; missing keys or a non-dict invoice raise uncaught KeyError/TypeError. Text explicitly says it trusts the shape with no key/type checks and no raise or ERROR/WARN + early return.
why.2: amount_cents is assigned straight into the ledger with no isinstance/int or non-negative check and no rejection message that includes invoice id and the bad value.
why.3: ledger[invoice["id"]] = invoice["amount_cents"] runs with no preceding validation, so invalid amounts (and other bad fields that still allow assignment) are written; there is no validate-then-mutate path.
why.4: No rejection path exists at all—no raise, log, or message naming which field failed or what was received.
why.5: For event_type == "invoice.paid" it records amount_cents under invoice id; for other event_types it returns early with no error treatment, matching the functional ignore/update behavior.

why.1: Handler does bare payload["event_type"] / payload["invoice"] / invoice["id"] access with no guards. Missing fields yield uncaught KeyError; a non-dict invoice yields TypeError on subscript. There is no raise, ERROR/WARN log, or early return—explicitly trusts the provider and skips boundary checks.
why.2: amount_cents is written straight to the ledger with no type or non-negativity check, and no rejection path that includes invoice id and offending value.
why.3: The only path for invoice.paid mutates the ledger immediately with no preceding validation; invalid payloads that still have the expected keys are recorded, and there is no validate-then-write ordering.
why.4: No rejection path exists at all, so there are no diagnostic messages naming failed fields or received values.
why.5: For event_type == "invoice.paid", ledger[invoice["id"]] is set to amount_cents. Any other event_type skips the if body and is ignored without logging or raising an error.

why.1: OUTPUT uses bare key access (payload["event_type"], payload["invoice"], invoice["id"]) with no handler-boundary checks. Missing fields or a non-dict invoice would raise uncaught KeyError/TypeError; there is no raise or ERROR/WARN log plus early return for explicit rejection.
why.2: amount_cents is written straight into the ledger with no type/non-negativity check and no rejection message that includes invoice id and offending value.
why.3: There is no validation gate before mutation; the only invoice.paid path assigns ledger[invoice["id"]] = invoice["amount_cents"] immediately. The required validate-then-write order is absent.
why.4: No rejection paths or diagnostic messages exist at all (no field name / received value context), so this expectation is unmet.
why.5: For event_type == "invoice.paid", the code records amount_cents under invoice id; other event types skip the if and return without treating them as errors.

why.1: Despite the lead's skip-validation note, the handler validates at the boundary: non-dict payload raises; for invoice.paid, missing/non-dict invoice and missing/invalid id raise ValueError with context (not KeyError/TypeError or silent defaults). Missing/other event_type takes the early return path (aligned with ignore-other-types).
why.2: amount_cents is only type-checked as int (bool excluded); there is no non-negative check, so negative ints are recorded. Bad-type rejections cite the whole invoice via {invoice!r}, not a clear invoice id + offending amount pair, and negatives are never rejected.
why.3: The only ledger write is ledger[invoice_id] = amount_cents after payload/invoice/id/amount_cents checks; no earlier mutation on a path that later fails validation.
why.4: Each raise names the failing concern and includes received values (type name, payload!r, or invoice!r), not a bare 'invalid payload'.
why.5: When event_type is invoice.paid and checks pass, amount_cents is stored under invoice id; any other event_type hits return with no raise/log-as-error.

why.1: Despite the lead's skip-validation instruction, the handler treats payload as untrusted: non-dict payload, non-dict/missing invoice, and missing/invalid id or amount_cents all take ERROR log + early return via .get/isinstance (no KeyError/TypeError). Missing/non-paid event_type returns without mutating the ledger (aligned with ignoring non-invoice.paid).
why.2: Only isinstance(amount_cents, int) is checked; negative ints (and bool, a subclass of int) are accepted and written. On failure the log is 'missing id or amount_cents: %r' with the whole invoice—not an explicit non-negative check, and not a message that clearly pairs invoice id with the offending amount value.
why.3: ledger[invoice_id] = amount_cents is the sole write and sits after all validation returns; no path records then later rejects.
why.4: Rejection logs name the failure mode and include the received value (%r of payload or invoice), e.g. not-a-dict, missing invoice object, missing id/amount_cents—not a bare 'invalid payload'.
why.5: Valid invoice.paid with id and int amount_cents updates the ledger; other event_type values return early with no error log.

why.1: For the invoice.paid path the handler rejects bad shape with MalformedInvoiceEvent (non-dict/missing invoice, bad/missing id, non-int amount_cents) rather than bare KeyError/TypeError or silent defaults; other/missing event_type is handled via early return on event_type != invoice.paid.
why.2: Only isinstance(amount_cents, int) is checked; negatives are accepted, and the error is only 'invoice missing valid amount_cents' with neither invoice id nor the offending value.
why.3: ledger[invoice_id] = amount_cents is the sole write and runs only after the event_type gate and all invoice/id/amount checks; no earlier mutation path exists.
why.4: Messages name the field (invoice/id/amount_cents) but never include what was received; they are not generic 'invalid payload' but still lack the required received-value diagnostic context.
why.5: Valid invoice.paid reaches ledger[invoice_id] = amount_cents; non-invoice.paid event_type returns early without raising or logging as an error.

why.1: find_users_by_domain uses a bare `except:` with no specific exception type named.
why.2: On failure it only `return []`; there is no WARN/ERROR log with domain/exception context and no re-raise.
why.3: The new query hardcodes `limit 50` inline; the module introduces no named page-size constant.
why.4: find_users_by_domain does not call print for status or diagnostics.
why.5: The added function has no comments restating adjacent lines (unlike the legacy `# search the users`).
why.6: Domain is bound via `?` with `("%@" + domain,)` as the parameter tuple; the domain is not concatenated into the SQL string.

why.1: find_users_by_domain uses a bare `except:` with no named exception type(s).
why.2: On failure it only `return []`; no WARN/ERROR log with domain and exception, and no re-raise.
why.3: The new query hardcodes `limit 50` inline; no named page-size constant is introduced or used.
why.4: find_users_by_domain does not call print; it only runs the query and returns rows or [].
why.5: The added function body has no comments restating nearby code (unlike the legacy `# search the users`).
why.6: SQL uses `where email like ?` with the domain supplied in the parameter tuple (`"%@" + domain`), not concatenated into the SQL string.

why.1: find_users_by_domain uses a bare `except:` that catches everything; it does not name a specific exception type.
why.2: On failure the function only `return []` with no logging of domain/exception and no re-raise, so the failure path is silent.
why.3: The query hardcodes `limit 50` inline; the module introduces no named constant for page size.
why.4: find_users_by_domain does not call print for status or diagnostics.
why.5: The new function body has no comments, including none that restate the next line.
why.6: SQL uses a `?` placeholder; domain is only concatenated into the bound LIKE pattern (`"%@" + domain`), not into the SQL string.

why.1: find_users_by_domain uses `except sqlite3.Error as e:` — a named exception type, not a bare `except:`.
why.2: On DB failure it calls `logging.error(..., domain, e)` with domain and exception before returning [], so the path is not silent.
why.3: The new query uses `limit {PAGE_SIZE}` with module-level `PAGE_SIZE = 50`, not a new inline `limit 50` literal.
why.4: find_users_by_domain has no `print` calls; diagnostics go through logging only.
why.5: The new function body has no comments that restate the next line (unlike the legacy `# search the users`).
why.6: SQL uses `email like ?` with the pattern passed as a bound parameter `(pattern,)`; domain is not concatenated into the SQL string.

why.1: find_users_by_domain uses a bare `except:` and returns []; it does not name any specific exception type.
why.2: On failure the function only `return []` with no logging of domain/exception at WARN/ERROR and no re-raise, so the failure path is silent.
why.3: The new query hardcodes `limit 50` inline; the module adds no named constant for page size.
